title: Operators
overview: Operators let you perform calculations, compare values, and make logical decisions. In this lecture you'll master Python's arithmetic, comparison, and logical operators.

blocks.title: Math in Python
blocks.body: Python can do all the math you learned in school. Arithmetic operators let you add, subtract, multiply, divide, and perform advanced operations.
bullets: // floor division — divides and rounds down
% modulo — returns the remainder of division
** power (exponent) — raises a number to a power

blocks.title: PEMDAS — Order of Operations
blocks.body: Python follows standard math rules: Parentheses, Exponents, Multiplication/Division, Addition/Subtraction.
bullets: Multiplication *, Division /, Floor Division //, Modulo % are next

blocks.title: Think Of PEMDAS As Math's Rulebook

leftPanel.label: Real Life Analogy
leftPanel.title: The Order Rulebook
leftPanel.description: PEMDAS tells Python which math operation to perform first

centerPanel.label: Programming Concept
centerPanel.textContent:
P → Parentheses first
E → Exponents next
MD → Multiply/Divide left to right
AS → Add/Subtract left to right

blocks.question: Does Python always calculate left to right no matter what?
blocks.answer: No! Python follows PEMDAS rules, not simple left-to-right. In 2 + 3 * 4, multiplication happens first (3 * 4 = 12), then addition (2 + 12 = 14). If you want addition first, use parentheses: (2 + 3) * 4 = 20.

blocks.question: What do you think 7 // 2 and 7 % 2 will give? How are they related?

blocks.title: Percentage Calculator
blocks.instructions: Ask the user for marks obtained and total marks. Calculate percentage = (obtained / total) * 100. Print with 2 decimal places.
blocks.starterCode:
obtained = int(input("Marks obtained: "))
total = int(input("Total marks: "))

percentage = 
print(f"Percentage: {percentage:.2f}%")
blocks.solution:
obtained = int(input("Marks obtained: "))
total = int(input("Total marks: "))
percentage = (obtained / total) * 100
print(f"Percentage: {percentage:.2f}%")
blocks.hint: Use (obtained / total) * 100. The :.2f in f-string rounds to 2 decimal places.

blocks.title: Comparing Values
blocks.body: **Comparison operators** compare two values and return either True or False.
bullets: <= less than or equal, >= greater than or equal

blocks.title: Comparison in Action
blocks.code:
print(5 == 5)
print(5 != 5)
print(10 > 5)
print(3 < 1)
print(7 >= 7)
print(8 <= 4)
blocks.expectedOutput:
True
False
True
False
True
False

blocks.title: Comparing Variables
blocks.code:
age = 18
voting_age = 18
print(age >= voting_age)
print(age == voting_age)

score = 85
passing = 70
print(score > passing)

blocks.question: What is the difference between = and == in Python?
blocks.answer: = is assignment (puts a value in a variable). == is comparison (checks if values are equal).

blocks.title: Eligibility Checker
blocks.instructions: Ask the user for their age. Check if they are eligible to vote (age >= 18). Print the result.
blocks.starterCode:
age = int(input("Enter your age: "))
eligible = 
print(f"Eligible to vote: {eligible}")
blocks.solution:
age = int(input("Enter your age: "))
eligible = age >= 18
print(f"Eligible to vote: {eligible}")
blocks.hint: Use age >= 18 as the comparison. This gives a boolean True or False.

blocks.title: Combining Conditions
blocks.body: **Logical operators** let you combine multiple conditions to make complex decisions.
bullets: and — True only if BOTH sides are True
or — True if AT LEAST ONE side is True
not — reverses the result (True becomes False)

blocks.title: Think of and/or as Gatekeepers
blocks.text: Two guards at a door. With and: both must say yes. With or: if either says yes, you enter. With not: the guard says the opposite.

blocks.title: Think Of and / or As Gatekeepers

leftPanel.label: Real Life Analogy
leftPanel.title: Gatekeepers
leftPanel.description: and = BOTH must pass. or = AT LEAST ONE must pass.

centerPanel.label: Programming Concept
centerPanel.textContent:
and: Both True → True
or: At least one True → True
not: True ↔ False

rightPanel.label: Code Example
rightPanel.code:
age = 20
has_id = True
print(age >= 18 and has_id)
print(age < 18 or not has_id)

blocks.question: If you are 17 years old (age = 17) and have an ID (has_id = True), does age >= 18 and has_id evaluate to True?
blocks.answer: No! The 'and' gatekeeper requires BOTH conditions to be True. age >= 18 is False (you're 17), so even though has_id is True, the whole expression is False. With 'or', only one needs to be True.

blocks.title: Logical Operators
blocks.code:
age = 20
has_id = True
print(age >= 18 and has_id)
print(age >= 18 or has_id)
print(not has_id)

score = 85
extra = False
print(score >= 80 or extra)

blocks.title: Scholarship Checker
blocks.instructions: Ask for marks and family income. Qualifies if marks >= 85 AND income < 50000.
blocks.starterCode:
marks = int(input("Marks: "))
income = int(input("Income: "))
qualifies = 
print(f"Qualifies: {qualifies}")
blocks.solution:
marks = int(input("Marks: "))
income = int(input("Income: "))
qualifies = marks >= 85 and income < 50000
print(f"Qualifies: {qualifies}")
blocks.hint: Use marks >= 85 and income < 50000 with the and operator.

keyTakeaways.text: PEMDAS rules apply — parentheses first, then exponents, etc.
Logical operators: and, or, not combine conditions
= is assignment, == is comparison — don't mix them up

whatYouLearned: All arithmetic operators including floor division and modulo
How PEMDAS determines the order of calculations
How to compare values with comparison operators
How to combine conditions with and, or, not
How to build a percentage calculator and eligibility checker
